397	
398	The CPU and RAM are massively different in speed:

400	```
401	CPU:   ~0.3 nanoseconds per operation

403	```

405	That's a **300x speed gap**. If the CPU had to go to RAM every time it needed a value, it would sit idle 99% of the time waiting. So the CPU has a **cache** — small, fast memory sitting right on the chip.

410	
411	When the CPU decides it needs something from RAM, it doesn't just fetch that one value. 414	RAM

416	                   ^CPU grabs this entire block at once
417	                   and stores it in cache
418	```

L1 fills up → oldest lines get demoted to L2

L1 miss → L2 miss → found in L3 → copy up to L1/L2, takes ~40 cycles
L1 miss → L2 miss → L3 miss → go to RAM, takes ~200 cycles
```

The same cache line can exist **in multiple levels simultaneously**. L2 is essentially a superset of L1 — if it's in L1 it's almost certainly in L2 and L3 too. The levels are just progressively larger safety nets catching data before it has to go all the way back to RAM.

They're just **labels pointing to string literals** defined in the text section. Essentially the assembly equivalent of C's string constants used in `printf`/`scanf`.

```asm
format1:  .string "a="
format2:  .string "%ld"
format3:  .string "a is %ld\n"
```

`format1`, `format2`, `format3` are just names — you could call them anything. They're labels that hold the **memory address** of where that string lives, exactly like a `char*` in C.

---

## The Equivalent C Code

```c
int main() {
    long a;
    
    printf("a=");              // format1
    scanf("%ld", &a);          // format2
    printf("a is %ld\n", a);  // format3
}
```

---

## Line by Line Breakdown

### printf("a=")
```asm
movq  $format1, %rdi    # first arg = pointer to "a=" string
movq  $0, %rax          # rax=0 means no floating point args (calling convention)
call  printf
```

### scanf("%ld", &a)
```asm
movq  $format2, %rdi    # first arg = pointer to "%ld" string
movq  $a, %rsi          # second arg = ADDRESS of a (not the value)
movq  $0, %rax          #
call  scanf
```

### printf("a is %ld\n", a)
```asm
movq  $format3, %rdi    # first arg = pointer to "a is %ld\n"
movq  $a, %rsi          # rsi = address of a
movq  (%rsi), %rsi      # dereference it — rsi = actual VALUE of a
movq  $0, %rax          #
call  printf
```

---

The `scanf` and final `printf` handle `a` differently:

**scanf** needs `&a` — the **address** — so it can write into it:
```asm
movq  $a, %rsi          # just the address, not dereferenced
```

**printf** needs the **value** of `a`, so it dereferences:
```asm
movq  $a, %rsi          # load address first
movq  (%rsi), %rsi      # then dereference to get the actual value
```

The `$` means "the address of", the `()` means "dereference" — same concept as `&a` vs `a` in C.

```

1456	call  printf

1458	                     # rsi = 42 again

1462	
1463	```asm

1465	pushq %rdi           # 8 bytes  → total 16, aligned
1466	call  printf

1468	popq  %rsi
1469	```

1471	If you only push one register (8 bytes), the stack is misaligned and you'll crash.

2587	## Why Virtual Address Spaces Exist
2588	
2589	This is the whole point of virtual memory — every process gets the illusion of its own private address space:
2590	
2591	```
2592	Program A thinks it owns all of memory from 0x0 to 0xffffffff
2593	Program B thinks it owns all of memory from 0x0 to 0xffffffff
2594	```
2595	
2596	They don't actually — the OS manages a mapping table (page table) that translates each process's virtual addresses to actual physical RAM locations. Two different virtual addresses in two different processes can point to the same physical RAM.

2600	## The One Exception — Writable Data
2601	
2602	The sharing only works for **read only code**. If a shared library has writable data (global variables inside the library), the OS uses a technique called **copy on write**:
2603	
2604	```
2605	initially:     A and B both point to same physical page
2606	program A writes to library's global variable
2607	OS intercepts: makes a private copy of that page just for A
2608	now:           A has its own modified copy, B still has original
2609	```
2610	
2611	So code pages are truly shared, data pages get privately copied the moment anyone writes to them.

Assistant:  ## Register Order for Variadic Functions (x86-64 System V ABI)

### Integer / Pointer Arguments
```
1st arg → %rdi
2nd arg → %rsi
3rd arg → %rdx
4th arg → %rcx
5th arg → %r8
6th arg → %r9
7th arg+ → pushed onto the stack
```

### Floating Point Arguments
```
1st float → %xmm0
2nd float → %xmm1
3rd float → %xmm2
4th float → %xmm3
5th float → %xmm4
6th float → %xmm5
7th float → %xmm6
8th float → %xmm7
9th float+ → pushed onto the stack
```

---

## For Printf Specifically

The format string always goes in `%rdi` since it's the first arg, then the rest fill in order:

```c
printf("a is %ld\n", a);
//      ^rdi          ^rsi
```

```c
printf("%d %d %d", x, y, z);
//      ^rdi       ^rsi ^rdx ^rcx
```

```c
printf("%f %f", 1.0, 2.0);
//      ^rdi    ^xmm0 ^xmm1
//      rax = 2  (telling printf there are 2 floats)
```

---

## Mixed Integer and Float

Integer and float registers are counted **independently**:

```c
printf("%d %f %d", x, 1.0, y);
//      ^rdi       ^rsi ^xmm0 ^rdx
//      rax = 1  (one float)
```

Integer args fill the integer registers, float args fill the xmm registers, completely separately, in order.
User: is this convention for the list of registers to pass to a function always teh same
Assistant:  Yes, the register order is always the same for any function call in x86-64 System V ABI (which is what Linux uses). It doesn't matter if it's `printf`, `scanf`, or any function you write yourself:

```
%rdi, %rsi, %rdx, %rcx, %r8, %r9 → always, in that order
```

---

## The One Special Rule for Variadic Functions

The only extra thing variadic functions like `printf` require is:

```asm
movq $0, %rax    # number of xmm float args
```

Regular non-variadic functions don't need this. So for example your `maxarray` function from earlier:

```c
long maxarray(long *arr, long n)
```

```asm
# caller sets up:
movq  $arr, %rdi    # first arg
movq  $n,   %rsi    # second arg
call  maxarray      # no rax needed, not variadic
```

No `%rax = 0` needed because `maxarray` takes fixed arguments, not variable ones.

---

## TL;DR

| Function type | Register order | rax needed? |
|---|---|---|
| Regular function | rdi, rsi, rdx, rcx, r8, r9 | No |
| Variadic (printf, scanf) | rdi, rsi, rdx, rcx, r8, r9 | Yes, set to number of float args |

Same register order always, variadic just adds the `%rax` contract on top.
User: ok, lets say you have more args than registers available, how does that work
Assistant:  When you run out of registers (more than 6 integer args or 8 float args), the extra args get **pushed onto the stack** in reverse order.

---

## Example

```c
myfunction(a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h);
//         1  2  3  4  5  6  7  8
```

```asm
movq  a, %rdi    # arg 1
movq  b, %rsi    # arg 2
movq  c, %rdx    # arg 3
movq  d, %rcx    # arg 4
movq  e, %r8     # arg 5
movq  f, %r9     # arg 6
pushq h          # arg 8 pushed FIRST (reverse order)
pushq g          # arg 7 pushed SECOND
call  myfunction
```

---

## Why Reverse Order

They're pushed in reverse so that when the function reads them off the stack, arg 7 is at the **lowest address** — meaning the function sees them in the correct left-to-right order:

```
Stack (grows downward)
|  arg 7  |  ← %rsp points here, function reads this first
|  arg 8  |
|  ...    |
```

---

## After the Call

The caller is responsible for **cleaning up the stack** after the call returns:

```asm
call  myfunction
addq  $16, %rsp    # pop the 2 stack args (2 × 8 bytes = 16)
```

---

## TL;DR

| Arg number | Where it goes |
|---|---|
| 1-6 (integers) | rdi, rsi, rdx, rcx, r8, r9 |
| 7+ (integers) | pushed onto stack in reverse order |
| 1-8 (floats) | xmm0-xmm7 |
| 9+ (floats) | pushed onto stack in reverse order |

Registers are faster so they get filled first, stack is the overflow bucket.

---

## How It Actually Works

The called function knows about stack arguments purely because the **function signature tells it how many args to expect**. It's baked in at compile/write time, not detected at runtime.

For a regular function:
```c
long myfunction(long a, long b, long c, long d, long e, long f, long g, long h)
```
The function was written knowing it has 8 args. It knows args 1-6 are in registers and args 7-8 are on the stack. It just reads them from the known stack offset:

```asm
# inside myfunction
# args 1-6 already in registers
movq  16(%rbp), %rax    # arg 7 is at this specific stack offset
movq  24(%rbp), %rax    # arg 8 is at this specific stack offset
```

The offsets are hardcoded — the function just knows where to look.

---

## For Printf Specifically

This is where `%rax` actually becomes important. Printf uses the **format string** to figure out how many args to expect and what type they are:

```c
printf("%d %f %ld", x, y, z)
```

Printf literally **parses the format string at runtime**, counting the `%` specifiers to know:
- How many args were passed
- What type each one is
- Whether to look in integer registers, xmm registers, or the stack

So for printf, the format string IS the mechanism for knowing what args exist. That's why passing the wrong format specifier for a type is so dangerous — printf will read the wrong register or wrong stack offset and get garbage.

---

## TL;DR

| Function type | How it knows about args |
|---|---|
| Regular function | Hardcoded offsets from function signature |
| Printf/variadic | Parses format string at runtime to count and type args |

There's no automatic detection — it's always either hardcoded knowledge or explicit runtime parsing of the format string.

The CPU has absolutely no idea it's even dealing with function arguments. It just sees:

```asm

                         # the fact that it's "arg 7" is a human concept
```

Someone literally wrote that parsing code inside printf. It's not a CPU or compiler feature, it's just a normal loop in C.

---

## TL;DR

- **Compiler** → figures out arg count, types, and locations at compile time
- **CPU** → has zero concept of args, just executes memory reads and register moves
- **Printf** → only apparent exception, but it's just a hand-written format string parser, not anything special about the CPU or compiler

Everything is baked in before the program ever runs. Runtime arg detection doesn't exist at the CPU level.

Assistant:  ## Printf's Runtime Parsing

Printf is declared as:

```c
int printf(const char *format, ...);
```

The `...` means variadic — it accepts any number of any type of arguments. The compiler has **no idea at compile time** how many args will be passed or what types they are. So printf has to figure it out itself at runtime by parsing the format string.

---

## What Printf Actually Does Internally

When you call:
```c
printf("%d %f %ld\n", x, y, z);
```

Printf receives the format string in `%rdi` and then literally does something like this internally:

```c
// simplified printf internals
char *p = format;
while (*p != '\0') {
    if (*p == '%') {
        p++;
        if (*p == 'd')       // integer, read next arg from integer registers
        else if (*p == 'f')  // float, read next arg from xmm registers
        else if (*p == 's')  // string, read next arg as pointer
    }
    p++;
}
```

It walks the format string **one character at a time**, and every time it hits a `%` specifier it knows to grab the next argument — from the integer registers, xmm registers, or stack depending on the type.

---

## The Internal Mechanism: va_list

Printf uses a special C mechanism called `va_list` to do this:

```c
int printf(const char *format, ...) {
    va_list args;
    va_start(args, format);    // sets up a pointer to the first variadic arg
    
    while (*format) {
        if (*format == '%') {
            format++;
            if (*format == 'd') {
                int val = va_arg(args, int);    // grab next int arg
                // print it
            }
            else if (*format == 'f') {
                double val = va_arg(args, double);  // grab next float arg
                // print it
            }
        }
        format++;
    }
    va_end(args);
}
```

`va_arg` is essentially just **advancing a pointer** through the registers and stack where the args were placed by the caller.

---

## Why rax Matters Here

You'd think the compiler could figure it out at the call site:

```c

```

And yes, the compiler **does** know at this specific call site there are 3 args. It will correctly set up the registers and stack. But the problem is **inside printf's own implementation**:

```c

Printf's body was compiled **separately**, long before your code ever called it. It lives in libc, already compiled into machine code. When its code was being compiled, there was no way to know how many args any future caller would pass.

---

## The Core Issue

Every call site can pass different numbers of args:

This slide introduces two types of libraries and how the linker handles them differently.

---

## Static Libraries (.a files)

The linker takes the library code and **physically copies it into your executable**:

```
main.o + libmath.a  →  executable (contains everything, self contained)
```

Your executable is bigger but **completely self contained**. It can run on any machine with no dependencies. This is why some programs "just work" when you copy them to another machine.

---

## Shared Libraries (.so files)

The linker does NOT copy the code in. It just **verifies the symbols exist** and leaves a reference:

```
main.o + libc.so  →  executable (just has "I need printf from libc.so")
```

The actual library code stays separate. At **runtime** when you actually run the program, the OS loads the shared library into memory and connects everything up.

```
runtime:
executable  +  libc.so loaded by OS  →  program runs
```

---

## Why The Distinction Matters

| | Static (.a) | Shared (.so) |
|---|---|---|
| Library copied into executable | Yes | No |
| Executable size | Bigger | Smaller |
| Dependencies at runtime | None | Needs .so file present |
| Memory usage | Each program has own copy | All programs share one copy in memory |
| Example | your own utility library | libc, libpthread |

**Static — every program gets its own copy:**

```
Program A running:    [A's code + printf copy 1 + malloc copy 1]
Program B running:    [B's code + printf copy 2 + malloc copy 2]
Program C running:    [C's code + printf copy 3 + malloc copy 3]
```

If 100 programs are running that all use printf, you have **100 copies of printf in RAM simultaneously**. Complete waste.

**Shared — everyone points to the same copy:**

```
Program A running:    [A's code] ──┐
Program B running:    [B's code] ──┤──→ [one copy of libc.so in RAM]
Program C running:    [C's code] ──┘
```

The OS loads libc.so into memory **once**, and every program that needs it just points to that same copy. 100 programs running = still just 1 copy of printf in RAM.

This is literally why libc is a shared library — virtually every C program on your system uses it, so sharing one copy saves enormous amounts of RAM.

## Do You Have to Specify Static or Shared?

Yes, you explicitly tell the compiler/linker which you want.

**Creating a static library:**
```bash
gcc -c mylib.c -o mylib.o      # compile to object file
ar rcs libmylib.a mylib.o      # package into static library
```

**Creating a shared library:**
```bash
gcc -c -fPIC mylib.c -o mylib.o    # compile with Position Independent Code flag
gcc -shared -o libmylib.so mylib.o  # package into shared library
```

The `-fPIC` flag is important for shared libraries — it tells the compiler to generate code that can be loaded at **any memory address**, since the OS decides where to load the shared library at runtime and it might be different every time.

**Linking against them:**
```bash
gcc main.c -L. -lmylib          # linker decides based on what it finds
gcc main.c -L. -static -lmylib  # force static linking
```

|---|---|---|
| RAM with 100 programs | 100 copies of library | 1 copy shared by all |
| Creation flag | `ar rcs` | `gcc -shared -fPIC` |

Static call:

i * 2  ==  i << 1

## What an OS Actually Needs

The OS kernel itself is written in C but uses almost **none of the standard C library**. The kernel can't use printf, malloc, or any libc functions because:

```
libc depends on the OS to function
OS kernel is what libc calls INTO
```

It's circular — libc calls the OS for memory, I/O, etc. The kernel IS the bottom of the stack, there's nothing below it to call.

So the kernel implements its own versions of everything it needs:

```c
// linux kernel has its own:
printk()       // instead of printf
kmalloc()      // instead of malloc
memcpy()       // reimplemented from scratch
strlen()       // reimplemented from scratch
```

---

## The Layers

```
user programs          → use libc (printf, malloc, etc.)
        ↓ calls
libc                   → translates to system calls
        ↓ calls
OS kernel              → handles actual hardware
        ↓ talks to
hardware
```

---

## Who Provides libc Then?

When building a complete OS, someone has to provide libc for user programs. The options are:

**Port an existing libc** — most OS developers just port an existing implementation:
- **glibc** — GNU's libc, used on Linux
- **musl** — lightweight alternative libc
- **newlib** — common for embedded/hobby OSes
- **bionic** — Android's custom libc

**Write your own** — some do write their own minimal libc, especially for embedded or hobby OSes where they want full control or minimal size.

**Use a language runtime** — some modern OSes (like some research OSes) skip C entirely and use Rust or other languages with their own standard libraries.
